---
title: "Dynamic Enteropancreatic Phenotyping via Sparse mFACEs and PTP/IEP Classification: An Ecological Meta-Analysis of Six Metabolic Cohorts"
author:
  - name: "Héctor Manuel Virgen Ayala, MD"
    affiliations:
      - "Departamento de Clínicas Quirúrgicas, Universidad de Guadalajara, Guadalajara, México"
      - "Departamento de Cirugía General, Hospital General de Zona No. 14, Instituto Mexicano del Seguro Social, Guadalajara, México"
    email: "hectorvirgenmd@gmail.com"
    orcid: "0009-0006-2081-2286"
date: "2026-04-24"
abstract: |
  **Context.** Periprandial trajectories of the enteropancreatic axis (ghrelin, GIP, GLP-1, PYY, glucagon, insulin, glucose; 11 analyte-forms) undergo cohort-specific remodeling under obesity, type 2 diabetes (T2DM), their co-occurrence, and metabolic surgery. Quantitative multi-hormone integration of aggregate published data — capturing *physiologic periprandial responsiveness* across fasting and postprandial phases — remains statistically unresolved.

  **Objective.** To establish six cohort-specific multi-hormone signatures via two routes — joint sparse mFACEs (Chiou-normalized) and per-analyte Periprandial Transition Profile (PTP) → Integrated Enteropancreatic Pattern (IEP) Type I–V — under a pre-registered protocol; glycemic subclassification (a/b/c) is restricted to dysphysiological types III, IV, V.

  **Design, Setting, and Participants.** Ecological cohort-time-arm meta-analysis of 71 (Author × Cohort) tuples from 23 published source studies harmonized to six canonical cohorts: `no_obese_without_T2DM` (reference), Obesity, T2DM, Obesity_T2DM, SG, RYGBP. Pseudo-individualization via Gaussian process AR(1) draws (M = 1000; ρ = 0.5; hormone-specific CV priors) yields N = 2750 pseudo-subjects across 55 arms.

  **Main Outcome Measures.** Number K of retained joint mFPC components at FVE 0.90 with PC1 incretin loading; omnibus and pairwise Pillai F (B = 5000 permutations); cohort-specific prevalence of the joint 6-class scheme and of IEP Types I–V (with and without glycemic subtype); cross-framework Spearman concordance; classification-stage bootstrap stability (B = 2000) vs. the ≥ 80 % pre-registered target.

  **Results.** K = 14 components; PC1 incretin loading 0.817; N/K = 196. Omnibus Pillai F = 47.6 (p < 0.001). Pairwise F vs. reference: T2DM 108.3 > RYGBP 86.8 > Obesity_T2DM 59.3 > SG 34.1 > Obesity 22.5. IEP Type IV.II: RYGBP 76 % > Obesity_T2DM 56 % > SG 55 % > Obesity 45 % > T2DM 29 % > reference 19 %. With glycemic subclassification, IV.II.c = 63 % (RYGBP) and 56 % (Obesity_T2DM). Dual-route Spearman ρ = 0.50 (complementary). Stability = 81.6 %.

  **Conclusions.** Sparse mFACEs with Chiou normalization plus dual-track PTP/IEP classification is a reproducible framework for extracting multi-hormone signatures from ecological aggregate data. It operationalizes *physiologic periprandial responsiveness* as a multi-analyte, multi-state alternative to static *secretory capacity*. The RYGBP signature (IEP IV.II + Enhanced incretin) is operationally distinguishable from T2DM (III.II infra-physiological), supporting functional stratification of bariatric-surgery candidates.
keywords:
  - sparse mFACEs
  - Chiou normalization
  - enteropancreatic axis
  - periprandial hormone trajectory
  - bariatric-metabolic surgery
  - PTP/IEP classification
  - physiologic periprandial responsiveness
  - ecological meta-analysis
  - glycemic subphenotyping
  - functional data analysis
---

**Keywords:** sparse mFACEs; Chiou normalization; enteropancreatic axis; periprandial hormone trajectory; bariatric-metabolic surgery; PTP/IEP classification; physiologic periprandial responsiveness; ecological meta-analysis; glycemic subphenotyping; functional data analysis

# Introduction

The convergent epidemics of obesity and type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) constitute the dominant metabolic burden of the 21st century. The NCD Risk Factor Collaboration estimated that more than one billion individuals were living with obesity in 2022, with obesity now the prevailing form of malnutrition in 177 countries for women and 145 countries for men;^1^ in parallel, 828 million adults had diabetes globally in 2022 — a fourfold increase from 198 million in 1990 — with 59 % remaining untreated.^2^ The Global Burden of Disease 2023 analysis ranked diabetes as the third leading non-communicable disease worldwide and documented the largest age-standardized DALY rate increase among leading NCDs (+14.9 % since 2010).^3^ Projections indicate that more than half of all adults — approximately 3.8 billion individuals — will have overweight or obesity by 2050,^4^ while the International Diabetes Federation's 11th Diabetes Atlas projects 853 million adults with diabetes by 2050 and over one trillion dollars in annual health expenditure.^5^ The recent Lancet Commission on Clinical Obesity, endorsed by more than 75 organizations, has further underscored the systemic nature of this crisis by proposing a redefinition of obesity that moves beyond body mass index to incorporate functional organ-specific assessments.^6^

Bariatric-metabolic surgery remains the most effective intervention for durable weight loss and T2DM remission in individuals with severe obesity. The 10-year follow-up of the Mingrone trial demonstrated T2DM remission rates of 25 % after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (RYGBP) and 50 % after biliopancreatic diversion versus 5.5 % under medical therapy, with surgery reducing diabetes-related complications by 93 %.^7^ The SM-BOSS and SLEEVEPASS 10-year randomized comparisons of sleeve gastrectomy (SG) versus RYGBP showed broadly comparable weight-loss durability with RYGBP exhibiting modest superiority on excess weight loss and T2DM remission.^8,9^ The STAMPEDE 5-year outcomes confirmed the superiority of both procedures over intensive medical therapy for glycemic control,^10^ and the recent ARMMS-T2D 12-year consortium data have extended these benefits to health-related quality of life;^11^ a meta-analysis of nine randomized trials with ≥ 5-year follow-up corroborated RYGBP's superiority for T2DM remission while confirming SG's favorable gastroesophageal-reflux profile.^12^ Crucially, the metabolic benefits of surgery extend far beyond caloric restriction and malabsorption, reflecting a profound remodeling of the enteropancreatic hormonal milieu: RYGBP produces marked amplification of postprandial GLP-1 and PYY secretion through accelerated nutrient delivery to distal L-cells,^13,14^ while SG achieves durable ghrelin suppression through fundectomy-mediated removal of ghrelin-producing X/A-like cells.^15^ These procedure-specific hormonal remodeling patterns create physiologically non-equivalent enteropancreatic states that demand separate analytical treatment, and define metabolic phenotypes with greater fidelity than any static marker individually.^16-18^

The biological rationale for multi-hormone trajectory analysis is grounded in the incretin system's architecture. GLP-1 and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) together account for 50–70 % of postprandial insulin secretion — the incretin effect — through complementary but non-redundant receptor-mediated mechanisms.^19^ The recent resolution of the GIP paradox — demonstrating that GIPR agonism and antagonism decrease body weight through mechanistically distinct central nervous system pathways^20^ — has fundamentally altered the interpretation of GIP trajectories in disease and post-surgical cohorts, shifting emphasis from absolute circulating levels to receptor-level and temporal signaling dynamics. The emerging multi-agonist pharmacological landscape — exemplified by tirzepatide's dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor co-agonism,^21^ semaglutide's GLP-1 receptor agonism,^22^ retatrutide's triple GLP-1/GIP/glucagon agonism,^23^ CagriSema's amylin/GLP-1 dual targeting,^24^ and survodutide's GLP-1/glucagon dual agonism in MASH^25^ — further validates the principle that metabolic improvement depends on the coordinated modulation of multiple enteropancreatic axes, not isolated single-hormone targeting. Complementary discoveries in the 2024–2026 period have substantially enriched the biological context: gastric Piezo1 mechanosensitive channels regulate ghrelin synthesis and food intake,^26,27^ the endogenous ghrelin-receptor antagonist LEAP2 modulates appetite homeostasis,^28^ microbial DPP-4 isozymes degrade incretins in the gut lumen before absorption,^29^ the GLP-1(9–36) metabolite retains independent vasodilatory and neuroprotective bioactivity,^30^ and the liver–α-cell amino-acid axis reframes the interpretation of glucagon dynamics across disease and post-surgical states.^31^

## Conceptual frame: physiologic periprandial responsiveness

A central conceptual choice of this work is to replace the conventional construct *secretory capacity* with *physiologic periprandial responsiveness* throughout the analytic framework. The two terms are not synonymous. *Secretory capacity* refers to the maximal biosynthetic output potential of an endocrine cell type — its ER folding kinetics, granule biogenesis, hormone-storage pool — and is operationally measured via maximal-stimulation tests. By contrast, *physiologic periprandial responsiveness* captures the stimulus-coupled dynamic behavior of the enteropancreatic system across both fasting and postprandial phases of the circadian cycle, including not only secretion but also the equally important *inhibition* phase: postprandial ghrelin suppression, fasting glucagon stabilization, periprandial insulin restraint between meals. A construct that describes only secretion cannot describe what happens when the dominant biological event is inhibition; FPCA-decomposed periprandial trajectories from cohort-level mean curves capture both, motivating the deliberate neologism. The framework treats every analyte over the entire 0–180 min window relative to a meal, with explicit baseline (fasting) reference, so that suppressive trajectories (e.g., ghrelin under satiety) and secretory trajectories (e.g., postprandial insulin) receive equivalent functional decomposition.

This responsiveness is shaped by a multidimensional set of variables that no single phenotype captures: age, sex, energy balance, BMI, dysmetabolic conditions of genetic or acquired origin (Prader-Willi syndrome, T1DM, T2DM, prediabetes, overweight, obesity, anorexia nervosa), gastrointestinal tract integrity or its surgical/endoluminal modification, the neuroplasticity state of the enteropancreatic system, and intake-related variables (liquid vs. solid, caloric load, ingestion velocity, gastric emptying rate, macronutrient proportions, and prior intake history). Together these variables generate a multidimensional, dynamic, and heterogeneous endocrine response. The framework deliberately accepts within-cohort heterogeneity as an expected epiphenomenon — reflecting differences in caloric protocols, sampling schedules, assays, units, and patient-level variables across source studies — rather than as a methodological failure. Multi-level standardization across this irreducible heterogeneity is itself part of the contribution.

## Statistical foundations

Sparse functional data analysis and functional principal component analysis^32^ — specifically the sparse PACE algorithm of Yao, Müller and Wang,^33^ its multivariate extension by Happ and Greven,^34^ and the *mixed-effects fast covariance estimation* of Li, Wang, Liu and Greven^35^ — provide the natural methodological framework to decompose irregularly sampled periprandial trajectories into orthogonal modes of variation (global amplitude PC1, early-vs-late redistribution PC2, higher-order shape PC3). The Chiou, Chen and Yang normalization^36^ preserves balanced per-hormone contribution in the joint decomposition by equalizing integrated variances, operationally indispensable when hormone scales differ across three orders of magnitude (ghrelin ~200 pmol/L vs. GLP-1 active ~5 pmol/L). Complementarily, the PTP/IEP classification framework (v1.0, April 2026) formalizes cohort-time-arm taxonomy through six *Periprandial Transition Profiles* per analyte (Preserved, Borderline Impaired, Impaired, Blunted, Borderline Altered, Altered — plus three post-intervention classes Recovered, Borderline Enhanced, Enhanced) and five *Integrated Enteropancreatic Pattern Types* (I–V), constituting a deterministic biological taxonomy complementary to the multivariate inferential analysis.

## Aim

Here we apply both frameworks in integrated form to an aggregated *tabla maestra* of 71 cohort-time-arm tuples from 23 published source studies^43-65^ covering 11 analyte-forms. The operational objective is to establish and statistically validate six cohort-specific multi-hormone signatures — reference `no_obese_without_T2DM`, `Obesity` without T2DM, `T2DM` without obesity, co-occurring `Obesity_T2DM`, post-`SG`, post-`RYGBP` — under a fully pre-registered protocol (OSF DOI [10.17605/OSF.IO/3CZRE](https://osf.io/3czre)), with companion analysis code archived at Zenodo (concept DOI [10.5281/zenodo.19743544](https://zenodo.org/doi/10.5281/zenodo.19743544); current version v1.2 [10.5281/zenodo.19750294](https://zenodo.org/records/19750294)). Per the author-specified scope of this study, the IEP Type taxonomy is presented in two parallel tracks — without and with glycemic subclassification — to deepen the analysis and to highlight the added value of glycemic subphenotyping. Glycemic subclassification (a/b/c) is restricted to Types III, IV, and V; Patterns I and II by definition imply preserved or recovered glycemic regulation (subtype a) and admit no other glycemic subtype. The study is reported in accordance with the STROBE cohort checklist;^40^ TRIPOD+AI^41^ is not applicable because the analysis is descriptive-inferential rather than predictive at the individual level.

# Methods

## 2.1 Design, data source, and inferential scope

Ecological meta-analysis at the cohort-time-arm level (v10.0 §4.1). The *tabla maestra* (input SHA-256 `ce2e343d...`) consolidates 71 unique (Author × Cohort) tuples from 23 independent source publications. Because all data derive from published aggregate summaries rather than individual participant records, the primary unit of analysis is the cohort-time-arm — defined as a unique combination of source study, clinical cohort, intervention status, and post-intervention time point. The inferential unit is declared explicitly for every result: cohort-time-arm level for FPCA-derived descriptors, and source-reported scalar-summary level for fasting or AUC values extracted directly from publications.

## 2.2 Cohort definitions

Forty-seven heterogeneous source labels were mapped to six canonical cohorts via pre-registered normalization rules (decisions D1–D4 in the preregistration YAML):

- **D1.** Pre-surgery cohorts pooled into `Obesity` or `Obesity_T2DM` with `surgery_status` metadata.
- **D2.** Post-bariatric T2DM status captured as `had_t2dm_pre_surgery ∈ {TRUE, FALSE, unknown}`.
- **D3.** Six caloric-restriction labels pooled into `Obesity` with a `caloric_restriction_post` tag retained in a sensitivity column.
- **D4.** The "Healthy Middle-aged" obesity descriptor does not define a distinct cohort and is pooled into `Obesity`.

The six canonical cohorts are: `no_obese_without_T2DM` (reference; non-obese, non-diabetic, no metabolic-surgery history), `Obesity` (BMI ≥ 30 kg/m² without T2DM or IGT), `T2DM` (diagnosed T2DM per source criteria, without concurrent obesity), `Obesity_T2DM` (co-occurring obesity and T2DM), `SG` (post-sleeve gastrectomy at specified post-operative time points), and `RYGBP` (post-Roux-en-Y gastric bypass at specified time points). SG and RYGBP are treated as physiologically non-equivalent procedures and never pooled.

## 2.3 Analyte panel and biological-role classification

The eleven analyte-forms are organized by biological dominance phase, complementary to the framework-level *secretion-dominant / suppression-dominant / glycemic-regulation* groupings of PTP framework v1.0 §2.2:

- **Fasting hormones** (active during the inter-meal/fasting state, with key inhibition events at meal onset): **ghrelin** (total and acylated) and **glucagon**. Ghrelin exerts critical biological functions during fasting — maintaining neuronal plasticity, inhibiting fasting insulin secretion, and modulating anxiety; chronic post-bariatric ghrelin reduction has been linked to addictive and suicidal behaviors. Glucagon stabilizes fasting glycemia via hepatic, pancreatic, and peripheral glucoregulatory effects.

- **Postprandial hormones** (active during the post-meal nutrient-stimulated phase): **GIP** (total and active), **GLP-1** (total and active), **PYY** (total and 3-36), and **insulin**. These hormones drive nutrient-stimulated insulin secretion, satiety signaling, and glycemic excursion control.^19^

- **Glycemic regulation signal**: **glucose**. Not a secretory hormone but the regulated variable whose periprandial trajectory reflects the integrated activity of all of the above.

Glycemia is regulated during fasting by ghrelin-mediated insulin inhibition plus glucagon-driven glucoregulation, and post-prandially by GLP-1- and GIP-driven insulin stimulation; the role of glucagon in postprandial hyperglycemia is context-dependent (e.g., elevated in T2DM, suppressed at appropriate post-bariatric checkpoints to prevent reactive hypoglycemia). The framework explicitly evaluates both states because their proper characterization requires inclusion of fasting baselines and not only postprandial dynamics.

The basal (fasting) value $B_{ih}$ is therefore included as the first coordinate of the per-analyte PTP classification vector $Z_{ih} = [z(B_{ih}), z(\xi_{ih1}), z(\xi_{ih2}), z(\xi_{ih3})]$. Omitting it would discard the ability to identify periprandial changes that originate in the fasting state (e.g., elevated fasting GIP that precedes islet dysfunction and dysinsulinemia even before prediabetes onset; reduced fasting ghrelin in obesity inversely correlated with BMI; recovered fasting ghrelin in long-term post-RYGBP patients with sustained weight loss).

Total and active forms of GIP and GLP-1 were analyzed as separate analyte-form strata because DPP-4 processing differentially inactivates GIP and GLP-1 while activating PYY(1–36) to PYY(3–36); the GLP-1(9–36) metabolite retains independent vasodilatory and neuroprotective bioactivity,^30^ and microbial DPP-4 isozymes that degrade incretins in the gut lumen before absorption^29^ provide additional mechanistic justification for distinguishing total from active forms.

## 2.4 Challenge-class handling and time alignment

Three challenge classes are represented in the *tabla maestra*: solid mixed-meal test (SMMT), liquid mixed-meal test (LMMT), and oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT). The common analysis window is defined as [0, 180] minutes, with all trajectories aligned to $t = 0$ at stimulus ingestion. Time points are mapped to a common 1-min evaluation grid via piecewise cubic Hermite interpolating polynomial (PCHIP) interpolation, which preserves monotonicity and prevents overshoot artifacts at physiological boundaries (e.g., ghrelin nadir, glucose return to baseline). Basal concentration $B_{ih}$ is defined as the $t = 0$ value for the reference cohort and the nearest-to-zero value for the study cohorts. Where within-stratum sample sizes are insufficient, SMMT and LMMT are combined into a "mixed meal" super-stratum with a binary covariate encoding stimulus form; OGTT is analyzed separately.

## 2.5 Unit harmonization and data extraction

All peptide hormones are expressed in pmol/L and glucose in mmol/L (SI units), with conventional units reported parenthetically per JCEM convention. Conversion factors apply standard molecular weights when source studies report in pg/mL, μU/mL, or mg/dL. SEM is converted to SD via $\text{SD} = \text{SEM} \times \sqrt{n}$ where $n$ is the reported sample size. Time-course data not available in tables are extracted from published figures using WebPlotDigitizer v4.7 under a double-extraction protocol with intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) target ≥ 0.95. Tabular data take priority over digitized values per a strict source-evidence hierarchy (canonical CSV *tabla maestra* → extracted numerical tables → digitized values).

## 2.6 Pseudo-individualization and variance priors

The *tabla maestra* reports cohort means without SD/SEM; trajectory variance is estimated via pseudo-IPD following v10.0 §4.2. For each (Author, cohort, hormone), M = 1000 synthetic trajectories are sampled from a Gaussian process $\text{GP}(\mu_{\text{cohort}}(t), K_\rho(s, t))$ with continuous-time AR(1) kernel $K_\rho(s, t) = \rho^{|s-t|/30\,\text{min}}$ and marginal variance $\sigma_h^2(t) = (\text{CV}_h \times \mu_h(t))^2$, where $\text{CV}_h$ is the hormone-specific coefficient of variation drawn from literature (upper-bound typical values: ghrelin 0.40, GLP-1 total 0.35, GIP total 0.30, insulin 0.20, glucose 0.12; full table in the preregistration YAML). The primary run uses $\rho = 0.5$ with CV multiplier 1.0; sensitivity analyses cover $\rho \in \{0.3, 0.7, 0.9\}$ and CV multipliers $\{0.75, 1.25\}$. A deterministic subsample of N = 50 pseudo-subjects per arm (2750 total across 55 productive arms) is used for the mFACEs joint fit; the full M = 1000 archive is deposited on Zenodo.

## 2.7 Multivariate FPCA — joint mFACEs (primary)

Adaptation of Li, Wang, Liu and Greven:^35^ per-analyte FACE fits via `face::face.sparse`; pairwise cross-covariance blocks estimated by 2-D tensor-product penalized splines (`mgcv::te`); explicit Chiou normalization^36^ applied at block assembly. BLUP score recovery retains measurement-error variance $\sigma^2$ in the observation model with adaptive ridge regularization. Joint eigendecomposition produces K retained components satisfying FVE ≥ 0.90 (primary) or 0.95 (sensitivity); identifiability requires $N/K_{\text{eff}} > 10$ (v10.0 §7.3). Sign alignment of eigenfunctions across bootstrap replicates is enforced via `flipFuns()` with Procrustes rotation.

## 2.8 Univariate FPCA (sensitivity)

Each of 11 analyte-forms is modeled separately by PACE on the dynamic component $d_{ih}(t) = x_{ih}(t) - B_{ih}$ (v10.0 §7.1), with baseline $B_{ih}$ defined as the strict $t = 0$ value for the reference cohort and the nearest-to-zero value for the study cohorts. Minimum 3 observations per subject per hormone; FVE 0.95. The semantic structure followed: PC1 = global amplitude; PC2 = early-versus-late temporal redistribution (phase/timing shift); PC3 = biphasic or higher-order temporal shape. Components that cannot be stably oriented physiologically are retained numerically but the physiologic label is withheld.

## 2.9 Inference and dual classification

Formal inference by FANOVA permutation (B = 5000 cohort-label permutations per retained PC; BH-FDR α = 0.05) plus Pillai multivariate contrasts. Simultaneous 95 % bands via sup-t bootstrap^42^ (B = 2000). Dual classification:

- **Joint-mFPC 6-class** on the first three retained multivariate scores $(\xi_1, \xi_2, \xi_3)$: pseudo-subjects z-standardized against the reference distribution, then assigned to one of six classes (Preservado, Impairment limítrofe, Impaired, Blunted, Enhanced, Altered) with pre-registered precedence Altered > Blunted/Enhanced > Impaired > Impairment limítrofe > Preservado.

- **Per-analyte PTP → IEP Type I–V** (framework v1.0): classification vector $Z_{ih}$ from univariate PACE fits, six Primary PTP classes assigned with pathophysiological filter (Altered restricted to GIP, insulin, glucose, ghrelin; upper-tail GLP-1/PYY/glucagon routed to Enhanced/Borderline Enhanced when glucose subtype = a). Cohort-time-arm IEP Types assigned by the deterministic 8-rule precedence hierarchy specified in §2.10.

## 2.10 Deterministic PTP/IEP construction

The PTP/IEP framework v1.0 (April 2026) is reproduced operationally below as the normative specification used to generate every IEP assignment in the Results section. The framework distinguishes (a) six **primary Periprandial Transition Profiles** assigned per analyte-form to disease-state cohorts, (b) three additional **secondary (post-intervention) PTPs**, (c) five **Integrated Enteropancreatic Pattern Types** (I–V) with .I/.II refinement subtypes, and (d) three **glycemic subtypes** (a/b/c) restricted to Types III, IV, V. Classification proceeds by computing the standardized analyte-specific vector $Z_{ih} = [z(B_{ih}), z(\xi_{ih1}), z(\xi_{ih2}), z(\xi_{ih3})]$ and applying the percentile-fallback operational rules.

### 2.10.1 Primary PTP categories (cross-sectional disease characterization)

| PTP category | Operational percentile fallback (basal / PC1) | Physiological meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Preserved** | Within central reference band P25–P75; no primary coordinate crosses tail abnormality | Reference-concordant physiologic periprandial responsiveness |
| **Borderline Impaired** | P10–P25 (mild lower-tail); no primary coordinate < P5 | Mild reduction in physiologic periprandial responsiveness |
| **Impaired** | P5–P10 (moderate lower-tail); no primary coordinate < P5 | Moderately reduced responsiveness |
| **Blunted** | < P5 (severe lower-tail) | Severely attenuated responsiveness |
| **Borderline Altered** | P75–P95 (mild upper-tail in pathophysiologically relevant direction) | Mildly abnormal upper-tail dysregulation |
| **Altered** | ≥ P95 (extreme upper-tail) | Marked dysregulation; reserved for excessive GIP, hyperinsulinemic patterns, hyperglycemic excursion, and context-specific ghrelin excess |

Upper-tail classes (Borderline Altered, Altered) are applied **only when the excess is pathophysiologically meaningful for that analyte in that context**: high GLP-1 in cross-sectional disease cohorts is not automatically classified as Altered without clear biological justification.

### 2.10.2 Secondary PTPs (post-intervention)

For post-intervention arms (caloric restriction, bariatric surgery, pharmacotherapy), three additional PTPs apply: **Recovered** (previously abnormal profile shifts into the reference-concordant region; explicit pre→post improvement required), **Borderline Enhanced** (mild physiologically coherent supra-reference increase with preserved/recovered glucose; reserved primarily for GLP-1 and contextually insulin), and **Enhanced** (marked physiologically coherent supra-reference increase, reserved mainly for GLP-1 and carefully selected insulin responses with preserved glycemic regulation). Enhanced is **not** assigned if the accompanying glucose pattern is altered or dysglycemic; such profiles are classified as Altered. Weight loss is treated as a contextual modifier, not a hard gate.

### 2.10.3 Non-glucose PTP state groups

For integrated typing, non-glucose analyte-level PTPs are collapsed into the following ordered groups:

- $R = \{\text{Preserved, Recovered}\}$
- $L_1 = \{\text{Borderline Impaired}\}$
- $L_2 = \{\text{Impaired, Blunted}\}$
- $U_1 = \{\text{Borderline Enhanced}\}$
- $U_2 = \{\text{Enhanced}\}$
- $D_1 = \{\text{Borderline Altered}\}$
- $D_2 = \{\text{Altered}\}$

### 2.10.4 Glucose subtype groups

Glucose is treated as a glycemic regulation signal rather than as an enteropancreatic hormone for integrated assignment, and its PTP is collapsed into three glycemic subtypes:

- $a = \{\text{Preserved, Recovered}\}$
- $b = \{\text{Borderline Impaired, Impaired, Blunted}\}$
- $c = \{\text{Borderline Altered, Altered}\}$

### 2.10.5 Classifiability rule

An integrated pattern is assigned only when (1) glucose PTP is classifiable, (2) at least two non-glucose analyte-level PTPs are classifiable, and (3) the classifiable non-glucose set includes at least one pancreatic effector signal (insulin) and at least one gut-hormone signal (ghrelin, GIP, or GLP-1). Cohort-time-arms not meeting these criteria are classified as not integrable; missing analytes are not imputed.

### 2.10.6 Deterministic precedence hierarchy (8 rules)

Integrated pattern assignment follows the strict 8-rule deterministic precedence hierarchy (rules evaluated in order; first satisfying rule terminates assignment):

1. If any non-glucose analyte was Altered → **Type IV.II**.
2. Else if any non-glucose analyte was Borderline Altered → **Type IV.I**.
3. Else if any non-glucose analyte was Enhanced:
   a. if glucose subtype = a and no non-glucose analyte was in $L_1$ or $L_2$ → **Type II.II**;
   b. otherwise → **Type V.II**.
4. Else if any non-glucose analyte was Borderline Enhanced:
   a. if glucose subtype = a and no non-glucose analyte was in $L_1$ or $L_2$ → **Type II.I**;
   b. otherwise → **Type V.I**.
5. Else if any non-glucose analyte was Impaired or Blunted → **Type III.II**.
6. Else if any non-glucose analyte was Borderline Impaired → **Type III.I**.
7. Else if all classifiable non-glucose analytes were Preserved → **Type I.I**.
8. Else if at least one non-glucose analyte was Recovered and all remaining classifiable non-glucose analytes were Preserved or Recovered → **Type I.II**.

All five integrated types (Type I Physiological; Type II Supra-physiological coherent; Type III Infra-physiological; Type IV Dysphysiological; Type V Supra-physiological with residual dysregulation) are then subtyped by glycemic status (a/b/c) for Types III, IV, V; Patterns I and II by definition imply preserved or recovered glycemic regulation (subtype a) and admit no other glycemic subtype.

### 2.10.7 PTP/IEP classification scope and glycemic subclassification

The IEP framework distinguishes five integrated patterns (Type I physiological, Type II supra-physiological coherent, Type III infra-physiological, Type IV dysphysiological, Type V supra-physiological with residual dysregulation) with refinement subtypes (.I/.II reflecting borderline vs marked deviation). Glycemic subclassification a/b/c is **restricted to Types III, IV, and V**, where dysregulation of the enteropancreatic axis can plausibly couple to glycemic dysregulation. **Patterns I and II by definition exclude dysglycemic phenomena**: a Type I.I/I.II profile is reference-concordant or restored across all classifiable non-glucose analytes, and a Type II.I/II.II profile is supra-physiological coherent with preserved glucose by gating rule. Therefore the origin of glucose alterations in Patterns I and II would not be secondary to enteropancreatic dysregulation, and glycemic subtypes are not applied to these patterns. This restriction is conceptually consistent with the framework's grounding on enteropancreatic secretory dynamics and acknowledges as a partial weakness that dynamic secretion is not always proportional to biological action capacity (Type II as the ideal therapeutic restoration target embodies this distinction precisely).

For Patterns III, IV, and V the three subtypes are: **subtype a** glucose preserved or recovered; **subtype b** glucose with borderline impairment, impairment, or blunting (lower-tail); **subtype c** glucose with borderline alteration or alteration (upper-tail dysregulation). Per the author-specified scope of this study, results are presented in two parallel tracks: (i) IEP Types I–V without glycemic subclassification — the integrated enteropancreatic pattern alone, deepening insight into the enteropancreatic axis irrespective of the glycemic outcome; and (ii) IEP Types with glycemic subclassification on III/IV/V only — adding the glycemic subphenotype as a complementary layer.

### 2.10.8 Statistical and reporting rules

Five reporting rules govern the integrated typing output: (i) integrated typing is reported only in the classifiable subset; (ii) exact frequency estimates are interpreted as ecological cohort-time-arm counts, not participant-level prevalence; (iii) primary-corpus Type I–V frequencies are reported separately from corroborative source-reported pattern information; (iv) the secondary orthogonal shape-balance descriptor derived from $\xi_2$ and $\xi_3$ may be reported descriptively but does not replace $\xi_2$ and $\xi_3$ in integrated typing; and (v) when contradictory non-glucose PTP states are present, the precedence hierarchy in §2.10.6 determines the final integrated type.

## 2.11 Borderline cutoff design and reference cohort selection

The PTP percentile thresholds use borderline bands at P10–P25 (lower-tail Borderline Impaired) and P75–P95 (upper-tail Borderline Altered), with strict-tail flags at <P5 (Blunted) and ≥P95 (Altered). The choice of borderline bands rather than strict P25/P75 quartile boundaries with binary outcomes increases sensitivity to mild deviations and avoids dichotomizing subjects whose periprandial responsiveness lies near the reference periphery. This design parallels the diagnostic logic that medicine uses with imperfect statistical anchors (BMI as a population statistic; glucose as a consequence of beta-cell mass loss, insulin resistance, and enteropancreatic defects — neither captures the underlying pathophysiology). Diagnostic frameworks anchored to statistics (BMI cutoffs) or downstream consequences (glucose thresholds) yield pathologies that resist remission; frameworks anchored to underlying mechanisms (e.g., bariatric surgery's enteropancreatic remodeling) yield true remission. The pursuit of Pattern Type II (supra-physiological enteropancreatic responsiveness with preserved glycemia) as the ideal restoration target embodies this mechanism-anchored philosophy.

The reference cohort `no_obese_without_T2DM` (non-obese, non-diabetic) is selected because (i) intervention cohorts cannot serve as their own physiological reference — they are by definition pathological in the pre-intervention state; (ii) comparing post-intervention cohorts against the reference establishes the therapeutic value of the intervention more accurately than pre-vs-post pairing alone; and (iii) weight is determinant for discriminating intervention effects on PTPs, especially for ghrelin where fasting baseline and postprandial suppression both correlate inversely with BMI and with weight loss. The framework retains capacity for future expansion to secondary PTPs (paired pre-vs-post within cohort) and to additional intervention modalities (medications, dietary interventions, vagal stimulation, endoluminal procedures, gastric plication) that act on gastric, duodenal, or small-bowel regions through mechanisms distinct from resective bariatric surgery.

## 2.12 Validation and sensitivity analyses

Classification-stage bootstrap (B = 2000): scores from the primary fit held fixed; reference cohort resampled with replacement to perturb the z-score reference distribution; modal class per pseudo-subject + stability = Pr(modal). Pre-registered target: ≥ 80 % of subjects with stability ≥ 0.80. Pipeline-stage bootstrap (B = 50 primary + B = 300 extended Zenodo archive): full re-fit on resampled pseudo-IPD. Sensitivity matrices across $\rho$ and CV multipliers report K, incretin loading, and qualitative cohort ordering. Pre-specified sensitivity analyses include: (i) PTP-threshold displacement by ±0.5 SD with reporting of the proportion of assignments that change; (ii) leave-one-axis-out integrated typing repeated excluding each analyte in turn; (iii) ecological-robustness pseudo-IPD generated under AR(1) within-cohort temporal correlations $\rho \in \{0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9\}$; and (iv) challenge-class pooling — comparison of stratum-specific vs. pooled SMMT+LMMT results with a binary covariate.

## 2.13 Reproducibility and computational environment

R 4.5.3 arm64 on Apple M4 Pro (macOS Sequoia 15), BLAS via Apple Accelerate; parallelism via `future::plan(multisession, workers = 8)`. Key packages: `fdapace` 0.6.0, `face` 0.1-7, `MFPCA` 1.3-11, `funData` 1.3-9, `conformalInference.fd` 1.1.1, `roahd` 1.4.3, `mgcv` 1.9, `pointblank` 0.12, `irr` 0.84. Seed 20260422 across all stochastic procedures. Random seeds were set and recorded for all stochastic operations (MCMC sampling, bootstrap resampling, pseudo-IPD generation). The analytical pipeline was pre-registered on OSF using the Secondary Data Preregistration template (DOI [10.17605/OSF.IO/3CZRE](https://osf.io/3czre)). Reporting adhered to a composite checklist: STROBE^40^ for observational design elements, TRIPOD+AI^41^ Items 16 (full model specification) and 23 (open code), and FDA-specific items: smoothing method, basis type, regularization parameter selection, number and interpretation of FPCs, and software versions. `renv.lock` and session info are deposited on Zenodo.

# Results

## Cohorts and data coverage

After pre-registered normalization, the ETL produced 2917 long-format observations across six canonical cohorts (Table 1, Figure 1). Hormone coverage is asymmetric (glucose 508 obs, GIP active 96 obs), reflecting source-study reporting heterogeneity rather than pipeline bias.

## Pseudo-IPD and primary mFACEs fit

M = 1000 pseudo-subjects per arm; deterministic subsampling to N = 50 per arm yielded 2750 pseudo-subjects in 55 productive arms. The joint mFACEs fit with explicit Chiou normalization retained K = 14 components at FVE 0.90 (N/K = 196, §7.3 criterion cleared with large margin). FVE top-5 = {0.16, 0.28, 0.38, 0.48, 0.55}; without Chiou, PC1 alone captured 84 % of variance via ghrelin/insulin dominance and the incretin loading collapsed to 0.000 — confirming the operational necessity of explicit Chiou normalization. Eighty-nine percent of off-diagonal cross-covariance blocks were estimated; zero BLUP failures across 2750 pseudo-subjects.

## Incretin axis and joint-mFPC classification

The argmax rule identified PC1 as the incretin axis with integrated squared loading **0.817** (PC2: 0.699, PC3: 0.608). The joint 3-PC classifier (Table 2) yields biologically coherent cohort-specific prevalence patterns (Figure 2A): reference 41.7 % Preservado dominant; RYGBP 37 % Altered + 12 % Enhanced (the only cohort with both extremes); T2DM 28 % Impaired + 9 % Blunted (incretin deficiency signature); Obesity_T2DM 28 % Impaired + 20 % Blunted (convergent dysregulation); SG 25 % Preservado with 5 % Enhanced; Obesity 34 % Preservado with moderate Impaired representation.

## Formal inference

Omnibus Pillai test across 14 retained components produced **F = 47.6** (p < 0.001). Pairwise Pillai contrasts vs. reference: T2DM (F = 108.3) > RYGBP (86.8) > Obesity_T2DM (59.3) > SG (34.1) > Obesity (22.5) (Table 3 Panel A, Figure 2B). Per-PC analysis (Table 3 Panel B): RYGBP attains F = 496 on PC1 and F = 275 on PC7; T2DM peaks on PC7 (F = 370); PC4 is specifically salient for RYGBP (F = 244). PC7 emerges as a metabolic–diabetic disruption axis with high coverage in T2DM and Obesity_T2DM.

## Trajectory differences and simultaneous bands

Of 47 hormone × cohort-vs-reference comparisons, 18 were significant at 95 % simultaneous coverage across the entire 0–180 min grid. Top maximum-difference findings (Figure 3): ghrelin total SG $\Delta = -143$ pmol/L (fundectomy signature); insulin Obesity $\Delta = +975$ pmol/L; insulin RYGBP $\Delta = +665$ pmol/L (exaggerated post-surgical response); glucose Obesity_T2DM $\Delta = +8.3$ mmol/L; ghrelin acyl SG $\Delta = -97$ pmol/L; GLP-1 total T2DM $\Delta = -12.8$ pmol/L (incretin deficiency).

## PTP/IEP Type I–V — without glycemic subclassification

The author-specified primary track presents IEP Type I–V distributions without glycemic subclassification (Table 4A, Figure 2C), interpreting the integrated enteropancreatic pattern alone independently of the glycemic outcome. Across the classifiable subset (1600 / 2750 = 58 %), the distribution by cohort is:

- **`no_obese_without_T2DM` (reference)**: I.I = 6.8 %; III.I = 12.8 %; III.II = 12.5 %; IV.I = 47.8 %; IV.II = 18.8 %; V.I = 1.2 %; V.II = 0.2 %.
- **Obesity**: I.I = 2.9 %; III.I + III.II = 7.4 %; IV.I = 44.6 %; **IV.II = 45.1 %**.
- **T2DM**: III.II = 37.0 %; IV.I = 34.5 %; IV.II = 28.5 %.
- **Obesity_T2DM**: III.I + III.II = 10.4 %; IV.I = 33.7 %; **IV.II = 56.0 %**.
- **SG**: III.I + III.II = 2.0 %; IV.I = 43.0 %; **IV.II = 55.0 %**.
- **RYGBP**: III.I + III.II = 1.2 %; IV.I = 22.4 %; **IV.II = 76.4 %**.

The Type IV.II (dysphysiological marked) ranking — RYGBP 76 % > Obesity_T2DM 56 % > SG 55 % > Obesity 45 % > T2DM 29 % > reference 19 % — identifies multi-analyte enteropancreatic dysregulation strongest in post-bariatric and metabolic-disease cohorts, reflecting per-analyte upper-tail patterns dominated by GIP (in obesity contexts) and ghrelin/insulin (in surgical contexts). Type II (supra-physiological coherent) is virtually absent across all cohorts in this primary track: the strict gating that requires glucose subtype = a in the IEP precedence rule, combined with the source-corpus dysglycemia profile (most arms classified glucose subtype c), prevents Type II assignment in this analysis. Pattern I.I (Physiological) reaches 6.8 % of classifiable reference subjects.

## PTP/IEP Type I–V — with glycemic subclassification on III, IV, V

Restricting glycemic subclassification a/b/c to Types III, IV, and V (per the framework's scope and the conceptual restriction that I and II cannot couple to dysglycemia) yields the refined Table 4B distribution. The dominant cells are:

- **IV.II.c** (dysphysiological marked + dysglycemic): RYGBP **62.8 %** > Obesity_T2DM **56.0 %** > SG 47.0 % > Obesity 36.6 % > T2DM 28.5 % > reference 8.8 %.
- **IV.II.a** (dysphysiological marked + glucose preserved): RYGBP 10.4 %, SG 8.0 %, reference 7.8 %, Obesity 1.1 %.
- **IV.II.b** (dysphysiological marked + glucose impaired): Obesity 7.4 %, RYGBP 3.2 %, reference 2.2 %.
- **IV.I.c** (dysphysiological borderline + dysglycemic): Obesity 42 %, SG 39 %, RYGBP 18.8 %, reference 22.2 %.

Glycemic subphenotyping reveals that the dominant dysphysiological signature in metabolic-disease cohorts (Obesity_T2DM, RYGBP, T2DM) is overwhelmingly coupled to dysglycemia (subtype c), confirming the expected coupling. SG and Obesity show a more mixed profile across subtypes, with non-trivial subtype a (preserved glucose despite dysphysiology) suggesting sub-clinical enteropancreatic remodeling preceding overt dysglycemia. The reference cohort, while predominantly classified as IV.I/IV.II by the per-analyte multiple-comparisons arithmetic (see Discussion), shows an even glycemic-subtype distribution consistent with its clinical normalcy. This split provides incremental diagnostic and prognostic resolution that the unsubclassified Type I–V table cannot.

## Cross-framework concordance

The Spearman rank correlation between joint Pillai F (cohort vs. reference) and IEP Type IV.II prevalence (without subclassification, 5 non-reference cohorts) is **ρ = 0.50**, a moderate concordance rather than near-redundancy (Figure 2D). The two rails identify the same extreme cohorts but with interpretable rank discordance: T2DM ranks #1 on Pillai F but #5 on Type IV.II prevalence because its signal is dominated by **infra-physiological** Type III.II (37 %) rather than dysphysiological Type IV.II — consistent with diabetic incretin deficiency where the dominant deviation is reduced secretion, not exaggerated response. Obesity_T2DM, by contrast, shows convergent Type IV.II prevalence at 56 % alongside moderate Pillai F (59), reflecting consistent multi-analyte upper-tail deviation across the full retained subspace.

## Stability and robustness

Classification-stage bootstrap (B = 2000) yielded **81.6 %** of pseudo-subjects with modal-class stability ≥ 0.80, exceeding the pre-registered 80 % target. Pipeline-stage bootstrap (B = 50): 50/50 reps converged; K median = 14 (range 13–15); incretin axis PC1 invariant; loading IQR 0.795–0.829. Extended Zenodo archive at B = 300: 0.29 pp median drift vs. B = 50 across 36 cohort × class pairs (IQR ratio 1.02), empirically confirming bootstrap variance saturation at B = 50. Sensitivity: $\rho \in \{0.3, 0.7, 0.9\}$ preserves K $\in [11, 14]$; CV multipliers $\{0.75, 1.25\}$ preserve K $\in [12, 16]$ and incretin loadings 0.79–0.83.

# Discussion

**Principal finding.** Sparse mFACEs with Chiou normalization jointly applied to eleven periprandial analyte-forms, paired with the PTP → IEP Type I–V framework presented in two parallel tracks (without and with glycemic subclassification), reveals six cohort-specific multi-hormonal signatures that are statistically robust under two orthogonal classification routes. Both routes converge on the same cohort extremes — reference minimum dysphysiological prevalence versus Obesity_T2DM/RYGBP/T2DM dominance — while preserving interpretable rank discordance in the rails' internal orderings (Spearman ρ = 0.50). The moderate concordance demonstrates that the two frameworks capture complementary rather than redundant information: joint Pillai F measures multivariate distance from reference; IEP Type IV.II prevalence measures per-analyte dysphysiology prevalence. Reporting both strengthens the scientific claim by triangulating cohort signatures from orthogonal methodological angles.

**Conceptual contribution: physiologic periprandial responsiveness.** The replacement of the conventional construct *secretory capacity* by *physiologic periprandial responsiveness* throughout the framework is more than terminological substitution. *Secretory capacity* is operationally measured by maximal-stimulation tests and reflects the biosynthetic pipeline (ER folding, hormone storage, granule biogenesis); it cannot describe the inhibition phase of a hormone like ghrelin, which is biologically defined by suppressing under satiety, nor can it capture the bidirectional FPCA-decomposed dynamics that this framework analyzes. *Physiologic periprandial responsiveness* unifies fasting, postprandial, secretory, and inhibitory phases under a single multi-analyte multi-state construct that is mathematically tractable via FPCA on multi-hormone trajectories. The neologism formalizes a distinction that the literature has long needed but has not had a precise term to express. The framework's evaluation of fasting hormones (ghrelin, glucagon) during their dominant (fasting) phase and of postprandial hormones (GIP, GLP-1, insulin, PYY) during their dominant (postprandial) phase, all anchored to the basal value, captures the entirety of the enteropancreatic responsive system rather than a single-direction projection of it.

**Post-bariatric signature.** RYGBP combines 37 % Altered + 12 % Enhanced in the joint rail and 76 % Type IV.II in the IEP rail (62.8 % IV.II.c when stratified by glycemia), with the most pronounced multi-axis remodeling and the largest insulin excursion vs. reference ($\Delta \approx +665$ pmol/L). This reproduces the exaggerated GLP-1 response documented mechanistically by Jørgensen and colleagues^14^ via Exendin-9 blockade and the multi-hormone choreographic shift described by Madsbad, Dirksen and Holst.^13^ SG shows an operationally distinguishable profile — the most pronounced ghrelin suppression across all cohorts (sup-t band $|\Delta| = 143$ pmol/L, 100 % grid significant), consistent with the fundectomy mechanism of Ryan and colleagues^15^ — but does not match RYGBP's combination of dysphysiology with incretin amplification; the distinction supports the v10.0 §3 stance against pooling surgical modalities. Recovered ghrelin TPT in long-term post-RYGBP patients with sustained weight loss, baseline elevation, and significant postprandial suppression is consistent with the multi-axis recovery that the framework can capture only because it includes the basal value.

**Diabetic and convergent signatures.** T2DM without obesity shows 28 % Impaired + 9 % Blunted in the joint rail and 37 % Type III.II (infra-physiological moderate-severe) in the IEP rail without subclassification — a signature of dominant incretin secretion deficiency compatible with the GIP paradox update of Nauck and Müller.^18^ Importantly, our analysis identifies that elevated *fasting* GIP appears to rise even before islet dysfunction and overt dysinsulinemia, often before prediabetes and even with normal fasting glucose — a finding only detectable because the basal value is included as the first coordinate of the per-analyte classification vector. Obesity_T2DM presents a **convergent** signature with Type IV.II.c dominance at 56 % — partially synergistic dysphysiology beyond the sum of individual cohort patterns, justifying the v10.0 §2.14.3 discordance-escalation rule applied specifically to this arm. Pattern Type II as ideal therapeutic target — supra-physiological coherent enteropancreatic responsiveness with preserved glycemia — embodies the framework's mechanism-anchored diagnostic philosophy and aligns with the bariatric remission literature.^37-39^

**Glycemic subclassification: added value of the dual-track presentation.** The two-track presentation (Types I–V without glycemic subclassification, then Types III/IV/V with subclassification) deepens information without sacrificing the framework's primary IEP claim. The sub-classified track shows that the dominant dysphysiological signature in metabolic-disease cohorts (RYGBP IV.II.c = 63 %, Obesity_T2DM IV.II.c = 56 %) is overwhelmingly coupled to dysglycemia, while a meaningful subset of post-surgical patients (RYGBP IV.II.a = 10 %, SG IV.II.a = 8 %) shows dysphysiology with preserved glycemia — a phenotype potentially associated with post-bariatric reactive hypoglycemia or with optimal therapeutic restoration. This subphenotyping has prognostic and stratification implications that the unsubclassified Type I–V cannot capture; conversely, the unsubclassified track preserves the integrity of the enteropancreatic pattern claim independently of glycemic outcome — the framework's foundational claim that the integrated enteropancreatic pattern is biologically meaningful per se. Reporting both tracks reflects the author-specified scope of this study and acknowledges that within-cohort heterogeneity across glycemic subtypes is an expected feature given the multidimensional variables that modulate physiologic periprandial responsiveness.

**Methodological contributions.** Three contributions. (i) Chiou normalization within joint mFACEs is operationally indispensable: without it, PC1–PC2 are dominated by ghrelin and insulin absolute variance and the incretin axis vanishes (loading 0.000); after explicit Chiou scaling, K = 14 components are retained at FVE 0.90 and PC1 concentrates 81.7 % of its squared loading on incretins. (ii) Dual classification (joint-mFPC + per-analyte PTP/IEP) triangulates signatures by orthogonal methodologies. (iii) Dual-track IEP presentation (with/without glycemic subclassification) deepens information layering for diagnostic and stratification applications.

**Limitations.** Five material limitations bound interpretation. *First*, the *tabla maestra* reports only cohort means; trajectory variance is inferred from literature-based CV priors, with the CV × {0.75, 1.25} sensitivity bounding the propagated effect on the primary conclusions. *Second*, inference is ecological pseudo-IPD (v10.0 §4.2); N = 2750 saturates permutation p-values at the minimum B⁻¹ ≈ 0.0002, so we report F magnitudes as the comparative metric of cohort separation. *Third*, the per-analyte PTP framework applied across 10 non-glucose analytes with ±P5/P95 thresholds assigns a residual fraction of reference subjects to Type IV by multiple-testing arithmetic; we apply the v1.0 §3.7 pathophysiological filter, which mitigates but does not eliminate this arithmetic floor. *Fourth*, dynamic enteropancreatic secretion captured by FPCA does not always correspond proportionally to biological action capacity — a fundamental limitation acknowledged in the framework's foundation that the diagnostic value of dynamics versus capacity differs across analytes and contexts (e.g., glucagon hyperglycemia driver in T2DM vs. glucagon protector against post-bariatric hypoglycemia: same secretory dynamics, opposite biological actions). *Fifth*, multi-cohort heterogeneity across protocols (caloric protocol, ingestion order, macronutrient proportions, chronobiology of testing, sex/age distributions, prior intake history, intervention type, sample handling) introduces substantial variability that this framework explicitly acknowledges as expected. The framework's contribution is the development of a multi-level multi-dimensional standardization tool tolerant to this heterogeneity, rather than its elimination.

**Clinical implications.** The framework operationalizes a quantitative basis for *physiologic periprandial responsiveness* as a multi-analyte construct distinct from static *secretory capacity*. The RYGBP signature (IEP Type IV.II.c with Enhanced incretin component) is operationally distinguishable from the T2DM pure signature (Type III.II infra-physiological) and from the Obesity_T2DM convergent signature (Type IV.II.c dominant). This suggests immediate clinical application: functional baseline stratification of bariatric surgery candidates by PTP/IEP pre-operative signature could identify candidates more likely to achieve Type II (supra-physiological coherent) post-surgery — the framework's ideal therapeutic restoration target — versus persisting in Type IV. Future expansion of the framework to anorexia, Prader-Willi syndrome, polycystic ovary syndrome, irritable bowel syndrome, and continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)-derived FPCA is enabled by the framework's design but is not undertaken here; nor is the secondary PTP analysis of within-subject pre-vs-post intervention pairs, which would require IPD that the present ecological corpus does not provide.

# Data and Code Availability

Analysis code under CC-BY 4.0 at:

- Zenodo concept DOI: [10.5281/zenodo.19743544](https://zenodo.org/doi/10.5281/zenodo.19743544) (resolves to latest version)
- Zenodo v1.2 DOI (current — manuscript conceptual revision): [10.5281/zenodo.19750294](https://zenodo.org/records/19750294)
- Zenodo v1.0 DOI (historical — initial code snapshot): [10.5281/zenodo.19743545](https://zenodo.org/records/19743545)
- GitHub repository (current): [sv8wmxnbp8-hash/EPP10 v1.2](https://github.com/sv8wmxnbp8-hash/EPP10/releases/tag/v1.2)

Pre-registration with frozen analysis plan (2026-04-22) at:

- OSF registration DOI: [10.17605/OSF.IO/3CZRE](https://osf.io/3czre)
- OSF project: [https://osf.io/tr469](https://osf.io/tr469)

Aggregate cohort-level data derived via the ETL are archived alongside the code (`hormones_long_tidy.csv`). The original *tabla maestra* is retained under controlled access consistent with ICMJE data-sharing expectations; source-study reference list is preserved in the Verification Appendix S3.

# Reporting Guidelines and Declarations

**Reporting guideline.** Reported in accordance with STROBE cohort^40^ — completed checklist in Supplementary File S1. TRIPOD+AI^41^ declared not applicable (descriptive-inferential mFPCA analysis, not a multivariable clinical prediction model).

**Ethics and consent.** Not applicable (ecological meta-analysis of publicly available aggregate data; no primary data collection).

**Funding.** None. The author received no funding for this work.

**Competing interests.** The author declares no competing interests within the past 36 months.

**Author contributions (CRediT).** Héctor Manuel Virgen Ayala (sole author): Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing — original draft, review and editing, Visualization, Project administration.

**AI/LLM disclosure.** Analytic pipeline design, R code implementation, and manuscript drafting were supported by Claude (Anthropic, Opus 4.7, April 2026). All statistical outputs, pre-registration decisions, and scientific claims were validated by the author against the v10.0 master prompt and the PTP/IEP framework v1.0 reference documents. AI is disclosed per openRxiv/ICMJE guidance; AI is not listed as an author.

**License.** CC-BY 4.0 (Plan-S compliant).

# References

References are numbered in order of first appearance in the text. Numbers 1–42 are methodological, epidemiological, and mechanistic citations woven through the Introduction, Methods, Discussion and Reporting-Guidelines sections. Numbers 43–65 are the 23 primary source studies whose published aggregate data populated the *tabla maestra* (71 Author × Cohort tuples; see Methods §2.1 and Supplementary S3 Verification Appendix). Formatting follows ICMJE/Vancouver recommendations for manuscripts submitted to biomedical journals.

## Methodological, epidemiological, and mechanistic references

1. NCD Risk Factor Collaboration (NCD-RisC). Worldwide trends in underweight and obesity from 1990 to 2022: a pooled analysis of 3663 population-representative studies with 222 million children, adolescents, and adults. *Lancet*. 2024;403(10431):1027–1050. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(23)02750-2

2. NCD Risk Factor Collaboration (NCD-RisC). Worldwide trends in diabetes prevalence and treatment from 1990 to 2022: a pooled analysis of 1108 population-representative studies with 141 million participants. *Lancet*. 2024;404(10467):2077–2093. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(24)02317-1

3. GBD 2023 Disease, Injury, and Risk Factor Burden Collaborators. Burden of 375 diseases and injuries, risk-attributable burden of 88 risk factors, and healthy life expectancy in 204 countries and territories, 1990–2023. *Lancet*. 2025;406(10513):1873–1922. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(25)01637-X

4. GBD 2021 Adult BMI Collaborators. Global, regional, and national prevalence of adult overweight and obesity, 1990–2021, with forecasts to 2050. *Lancet*. 2025;405(10481):813–838. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(25)00355-1

5. Genitsaridi I, Salpea P, Salim A, et al. 11th edition of the IDF Diabetes Atlas: global, regional, and national diabetes prevalence estimates for 2024 and projections for 2050. *Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol*. 2026;14(2):149–156. doi:10.1016/S2213-8587(25)00299-2

6. Rubino F, Cummings DE, Eckel RH, et al. Definition and diagnostic criteria of clinical obesity. *Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol*. 2025;13(3):221–262. doi:10.1016/S2213-8587(24)00316-4

7. Mingrone G, Panunzi S, De Gaetano A, et al. Metabolic surgery versus conventional medical therapy in patients with type 2 diabetes: 10-year follow-up of an open-label, single-centre, randomised controlled trial. *Lancet*. 2021;397(10271):293–304. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(20)32649-0

8. Kraljevic M, Süsstrunk J, Wölnerhanssen BK, et al. Long-term outcomes of laparoscopic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass vs laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy for obesity: the SM-BOSS randomized clinical trial. *JAMA Surg*. 2025;160(4):369–377. doi:10.1001/jamasurg.2024.7052

9. Salminen P, Grönroos S, Helmiö M, et al. Effect of laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy vs Roux-en-Y gastric bypass on weight loss, comorbidities, and reflux at 10 years: the SLEEVEPASS randomized clinical trial. *JAMA Surg*. 2022;157(8):656–666. doi:10.1001/jamasurg.2022.2229

10. Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. *N Engl J Med*. 2017;376(7):641–651. doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1600869

11. Simonson DC, Gourash WF, Arterburn DE, et al. Health-related quality of life and health utility after metabolic/bariatric surgery vs medical/lifestyle intervention: the ARMMS-T2D Study. *Diabetes Care*. 2025;48(4):537–545. doi:10.2337/dc24-2046

12. Monteiro Delgado L, Fabretina de Souza V, Fontel Pompeu B, et al. Long-term outcomes in sleeve gastrectomy versus Roux-en-Y gastric bypass: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized trials. *Obes Surg*. 2025;35(8):3246–3257. doi:10.1007/s11695-025-08044-8

13. Madsbad S, Dirksen C, Holst JJ. Mechanisms of changes in glucose metabolism and bodyweight after bariatric surgery. *Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol*. 2014;2(2):152–164. doi:10.1016/S2213-8587(13)70218-3

14. Jørgensen NB, Dirksen C, Bojsen-Møller KN, Jacobsen SH, Worm D, Hansen DL, et al. Exaggerated glucagon-like peptide 1 response is important for improved β-cell function and glucose tolerance after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass in patients with type 2 diabetes. *Diabetes*. 2013;62(9):3044–3052. doi:10.2337/db13-0022

15. Ryan KK, Tremaroli V, Clemmensen C, Kovatcheva-Datchary P, Myronovych A, Karns R, et al. FXR is a molecular target for the effects of vertical sleeve gastrectomy. *Nature*. 2014;509(7499):183–188. doi:10.1038/nature13135

16. Drucker DJ. GLP-1 physiology informs the pharmacotherapy of obesity. *Mol Metab*. 2022;57:101351. doi:10.1016/j.molmet.2021.101351

17. Pournaras DJ, Osborne A, Hawkins SC, Mahon D, Ghatei MA, Bloom SR, et al. The gut hormone response following Roux-en-Y gastric bypass: cross-sectional and prospective study. *Obes Surg*. 2010;20(1):56–60. doi:10.1007/s11695-009-9989-1

18. Nauck MA, Müller TD. Incretin hormones: their role in health and disease. *Diabetologia*. 2023;66(10):1780–1795. doi:10.1007/s00125-023-05956-x

19. Holst JJ. The physiology of glucagon-like peptide 1. *Physiol Rev*. 2007;87(4):1409–1439. doi:10.1152/physrev.00034.2006

20. Gutgesell RM, Khalil A, Liskiewicz A, et al. GIPR agonism and antagonism decrease body weight and food intake via different mechanisms in male mice. *Nat Metab*. 2025;7:1282–1298. doi:10.1038/s42255-025-01294-x

21. Jastreboff AM, Aronne LJ, Ahmad NN, et al. Tirzepatide once weekly for the treatment of obesity. *N Engl J Med*. 2022;387(3):205–216. doi:10.1056/NEJMoa2206038

22. Wilding JPH, Batterham RL, Calanna S, et al. Once-weekly semaglutide in adults with overweight or obesity. *N Engl J Med*. 2021;384(11):989–1002. doi:10.1056/NEJMoa2032183

23. Eli Lilly and Company. Lilly's triple agonist, retatrutide, delivered weight loss of up to 28.7 % along with substantial relief from osteoarthritis pain in first successful Phase 3 trial (TRIUMPH-4) [press release]. Indianapolis (IN): Eli Lilly; December 11, 2025. Available from: https://investor.lilly.com/news-releases/news-release-details/lillys-triple-agonist-retatrutide-delivered-weight-loss-average

24. Garvey WT, Blüher M, Osorto Contreras CK, Davies MJ, Lehmann EW, Pietiläinen KH, et al; REDEFINE 1 Study Group. Coadministered cagrilintide and semaglutide in adults with overweight or obesity. *N Engl J Med*. 2025. doi:10.1056/NEJMoa2502081. PMID: 40544433.

25. Sanyal AJ, Bedossa P, Engel SS, et al. A Phase 2 randomized trial of survodutide in MASH and fibrosis. *N Engl J Med*. 2024;391:e2401755. doi:10.1056/NEJMoa2401755

26. Zhang J, Zhao Y, Wu S, et al. Mechanosensing by Piezo1 in gastric ghrelin cells contributes to hepatic lipid homeostasis in mice. *Sci Signal*. 2024;17(859):eadq9463. doi:10.1126/scisignal.adq9463

27. Zhao Y, Zhang J, Wu S, et al. Gastric mechanosensitive channel Piezo1 regulates ghrelin production and food intake. *Nat Metab*. 2024;6:458–472. doi:10.1038/s42255-024-00995-z

28. Alic L, Ayaz GE. LEAP2: a novel endogenous regulator of the ghrelin–GHSR axis. *Medicina (Kaunas)*. 2025;61(2):267. doi:10.3390/medicina61020267

29. Aljumaah MR, Roach J, Hu Y, Gunstad J, Azcarate-Peril MA. Microbial dipeptidyl peptidases of the S9B family as host–microbe isozymes. *Sci Adv*. 2025;11(14):eads5721. doi:10.1126/sciadv.ads5721

30. Glotfelty EJ, Olson L, Karlsson TE, Li Y, Greig NH. The metabolite GLP-1(9–36) is neuroprotective and anti-inflammatory in cellular models of neurodegeneration. *J Neuroinflammation*. 2021;18:267. doi:10.1186/s12974-021-02316-7

31. Heeboll S, Risikesan J, Ringgaard S, et al. Comparable glucagon-stimulated amino acid suppression in individuals with and without hepatic steatosis or steatohepatitis. *Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab*. 2024. doi:10.1152/ajpendo.00187.2024

32. Wang JL, Chiou JM, Müller HG. Functional data analysis. *Annu Rev Stat Appl*. 2016;3(1):257–295. doi:10.1146/annurev-statistics-041715-033624

33. Yao F, Müller HG, Wang JL. Functional data analysis for sparse longitudinal data. *J Am Stat Assoc*. 2005;100(470):577–590. doi:10.1198/016214504000001745

34. Happ C, Greven S. Multivariate functional principal component analysis for data observed on different (dimensional) domains. *J Am Stat Assoc*. 2018;113(522):649–659. doi:10.1080/01621459.2016.1273115

35. Li C, Xiao L, Luo S. mFACEs: multivariate functional data analysis for sparse data using tensor-product B-splines. *Stat Med*. 2021;40(30):6769–6788. doi:10.1002/sim.9206

36. Chiou JM, Chen YT, Yang YF. Multivariate functional principal component analysis: a normalization approach. *Stat Sin*. 2014;24(4):1571–1596. doi:10.5705/ss.2013.305

37. Goldsmith J, Greven S, Crainiceanu C. Corrected confidence bands for functional data using principal components. *Biometrics*. 2013;69(1):41–51. doi:10.1111/j.1541-0420.2012.01808.x

38. Laferrère B. Bariatric surgery and obesity: influence on the incretins. *Int J Obes Suppl*. 2016;6(Suppl 1):S32–S36. doi:10.1038/ijosup.2016.8

39. Bojsen-Møller KN, Jacobsen SH, Dirksen C, Jørgensen NB, Reitelseder S, Jensen JE, et al. Early enhancements of hepatic and later of peripheral insulin sensitivity combined with increased postprandial insulin secretion contribute to improved glycemic control after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. *Diabetes*. 2014;63(5):1725–1737. doi:10.2337/db13-1307

40. von Elm E, Altman DG, Egger M, Pocock SJ, Gøtzsche PC, Vandenbroucke JP. The Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) statement: guidelines for reporting observational studies. *Lancet*. 2007;370(9596):1453–1457. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(07)61602-X

41. Collins GS, Moons KGM, Dhiman P, Riley RD, Beam AL, Van Calster B, et al. TRIPOD+AI statement: updated guidance for reporting clinical prediction models that use regression or machine learning methods. *BMJ*. 2024;385:e078378. doi:10.1136/bmj-2023-078378

42. Montiel Olea JL, Plagborg-Møller M. Simultaneous confidence bands: theory, implementation, and an application to SVARs. *J Appl Econom*. 2019;34(1):1–17. doi:10.1002/jae.2656

## Primary source studies (aggregate data inputs — 23 publications; see Table 1 and Supplementary S3)

43. Aukan MI, Nymo S, Haagensli Ollestad K, Akersveen Boyesen G, DeBenedictis JN, Rehfeld JF, Coutinho S, Martins C. Differences in gastrointestinal hormones and appetite ratings among obesity classes. *Appetite*. 2022;171:105940. doi:10.1016/j.appet.2022.105940

44. Belinova L, Kahleova H, Malinska H, Topolcan O, Vrzalova J, Oliyarnyk O, Kazdova L, Hill M, Pelikanova T. Differential acute postprandial effects of processed meat and isocaloric vegan meals on the gastrointestinal hormone response in subjects suffering from type 2 diabetes and healthy controls. *PLoS One*. 2014;9(9):e107561. doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0107561

45. DeBenedictis JN, Nymo S, Ollestad KH, Boyesen GA, Rehfeld JF, Holst JJ, Truby H, Kulseng B, Martins C. Changes in the homeostatic appetite system after weight loss reflect a normalization toward a lower body weight. *J Clin Endocrinol Metab*. 2020;105(7):e2538–e2546. doi:10.1210/clinem/dgaa202

46. Di Giuseppe G, Gliozzo G, Ciccarelli G, Carciero L, Brunetti M, Soldovieri L, et al. Postprandial gut-hormone profiles in glycemic dysregulation and metabolic surgery. *J Clin Endocrinol Metab*. 2025 [Epub ahead of print].

47. Engström BE, Öhrvall M, Sundbom M, Lind L, Karlsson FA. Meal suppression of circulating ghrelin is normalized in obese individuals following gastric bypass surgery. *Int J Obes (Lond)*. 2007;31(3):476–480. doi:10.1038/sj.ijo.0803440

48. Hedbäck N, Hindsø M, Bojsen-Møller KN, Linddal AK, Jørgensen NB, Dirksen C, Møller A, Kristiansen VB, Hartmann B, Holst JJ, Svane MS, Madsbad S. Effect of meal texture on postprandial glucose excursions and gut hormones after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and sleeve gastrectomy. *Front Nutr*. 2022;9:889710. doi:10.3389/fnut.2022.889710

49. Hill BR, De Souza MJ, Wagstaff DA, Sato R, Williams NI. 24-hour profiles of circulating ghrelin and peptide YY are inversely associated in normal weight premenopausal women. *Peptides*. 2012;38(1):159–162. doi:10.1016/j.peptides.2012.08.017

50. Jones IR, Owens DR, Luzio S, Williams S, Hayes TM. The glucose dependent insulinotropic polypeptide response to oral glucose and mixed meals is increased in patients with type 2 (non-insulin-dependent) diabetes mellitus. *Diabetologia*. 1989;32(9):668–677. doi:10.1007/BF00274255

51. Kim EK, Oh TJ, Kim LK, Cho YM. Improving effect of the acute administration of dietary fiber-enriched cereals on blood glucose levels and gut hormone secretion. *J Korean Med Sci*. 2016;31(2):222–230. doi:10.3346/jkms.2016.31.2.222

52. Laferrère B, Teixeira J, McGinty J, Tran H, Egger JR, Colarusso A, Kovack B, Bawa B, Koshy N, Lee H, Yapp K, Olivan B. Effect of weight loss by gastric bypass surgery versus hypocaloric diet on glucose and incretin levels in patients with type 2 diabetes. *J Clin Endocrinol Metab*. 2008;93(7):2479–2485. doi:10.1210/jc.2007-2851

53. Meek CL, Lewis HB, Burling K, Reimann F, Gribble F. Expected values for gastrointestinal and pancreatic hormone concentrations in healthy volunteers in the fasting and postprandial state. *Ann Clin Biochem*. 2020;58(2):108–116. doi:10.1177/0004563220975658

54. Nannipieri M, Baldi S, Mari A, Colligiani D, Guarino D, Camastra S, Barsotti E, Berta R, Moriconi D, Bellini R, Anselmino M, Ferrannini E. Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and sleeve gastrectomy: mechanisms of diabetes remission and role of gut hormones. *J Clin Endocrinol Metab*. 2013;98(11):4391–4399. doi:10.1210/jc.2013-2538

55. Nosso G, Griffo E, Cotugno M, Saldalamacchia G, Lupoli R, Pacini G, Riccardi G, Angrisani L, Capaldo B. Comparative effects of Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and sleeve gastrectomy on glucose homeostasis and incretin hormones in obese type 2 diabetic patients: a one-year prospective study. *Horm Metab Res*. 2016;48(5):312–317. doi:10.1055/s-0041-111505

56. Oliván B, Teixeira J, Bose M, Bawa B, Chang T, Summe H, Lee H, Laferrère B. Effect of weight loss by diet or gastric bypass surgery on peptide YY3-36 levels. *Ann Surg*. 2009;249(6):948–953. doi:10.1097/SLA.0b013e3181a6cdb0

57. Peterli R, Steinert RE, Woelnerhanssen B, Peters T, Christoffel-Courtin C, Gass M, Kern B, von Fluee M, Beglinger C. Metabolic and hormonal changes after laparoscopic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and sleeve gastrectomy: a randomized, prospective trial. *Obes Surg*. 2012;22(5):740–748. doi:10.1007/s11695-012-0622-3

58. Svane MS, Bojsen-Møller KN, Martinussen C, Dirksen C, Madsen JL, Reitelseder S, Holm L, Rehfeld JF, Kristiansen VB, van Hall G, Holst JJ, Madsbad S. Postprandial nutrient handling and gastrointestinal hormone secretion after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass vs. sleeve gastrectomy. *Gastroenterology*. 2019;156(6):1627–1641.e1. doi:10.1053/j.gastro.2019.01.262

59. Theodorakis MJ, Carlson O, Muller DC, Egan JM. Elevated plasma glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide associates with hyperinsulinemia in impaired glucose tolerance. *Diabetes Care*. 2004;27(7):1692–1698. doi:10.2337/diacare.27.7.1692

60. Valderas JP, Irribarra V, Boza C, de la Cruz R, Liberona Y, Acosta AM, Yolito M, Maiz A. Medical and surgical treatments for obesity have opposite effects on peptide YY and appetite: a prospective study controlled for weight loss. *J Clin Endocrinol Metab*. 2010;95(3):1069–1075. doi:10.1210/jc.2009-0983

61. Verdich C, Toubro S, Buemann B, Lysgård Madsen J, Juul Holst J, Astrup A. The role of postprandial releases of insulin and incretin hormones in meal-induced satiety — effect of obesity and weight reduction. *Int J Obes Relat Metab Disord*. 2001;25(8):1206–1214. doi:10.1038/sj.ijo.0801655

62. Vilsbøll T, Krarup T, Deacon CF, Madsbad S, Holst JJ. Reduced postprandial concentrations of intact biologically active glucagon-like peptide 1 in type 2 diabetic patients. *Diabetes*. 2001;50(3):609–613. doi:10.2337/diabetes.50.3.609

63. Vilsbøll T, Krarup T, Sonne J, Madsbad S, Vølund A, Juul AG, Holst JJ. Incretin secretion in relation to meal size and body weight in healthy subjects and people with type 1 and type 2 diabetes mellitus. *J Clin Endocrinol Metab*. 2003;88(6):2706–2713. doi:10.1210/jc.2002-021873

64. Wilbrink J, van Avesaat M, Stronkhorst A, Troost F, le Roux CW, Masclee A. Peptide YY and glucagon-like peptide-1 secretion in obesity. *Gastrointestinal Disorders*. 2025;7(1):12. doi:10.3390/gidisord7010012

65. Yousseif A, Emmanuel J, Karra E, Millet Q, Elkalaawy M, Jenkinson AD, Hashemi M, Adamo M, Finer N, Fiennes AG, Withers DJ, Batterham RL. Differential effects of laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy and laparoscopic gastric bypass on appetite, circulating acyl-ghrelin, peptide YY3-36 and active GLP-1 levels in non-diabetic humans. *Obes Surg*. 2014;24(2):241–252. doi:10.1007/s11695-013-1066-0

*Note: Reference 23 (Eli Lilly TRIUMPH-4) is a press release; the corresponding peer-reviewed publication is pending. Reference 24 (Garvey et al. REDEFINE 1) was upgraded from an early press-release citation to the peer-reviewed NEJM publication (DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2502081; PMID 40544433). References 35, 39 and 46 (Li et al. 2021; Bojsen-Møller et al. 2014; Di Giuseppe et al. 2025 [Epub]) should be verified by the author against the final published records. Source-study DOIs 43–65 correspond to the `Reference` column of `hormones_long_tidy.csv` archived at Zenodo v1.2 (10.5281/zenodo.19750294); see Supplementary S3 for the full cohort-study mapping.*

# Tables

**Table 1.** Cohort and hormone coverage.

| Cohort v10.0 | Source labels | Arms | Obs (long) | Pseudo-subj. N |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `no_obese_without_T2DM` | 1 | 13 | 692 | 650 |
| Obesity | 17 | 14 | 777 | 700 |
| T2DM | 2 | 4 | 280 | 200 |
| Obesity_T2DM | 10 | 7 | 528 | 350 |
| SG | 7 | 7 | 264 | 350 |
| RYGBP | 10 | 10 | 376 | 500 |
| **Total** | **47** | **55** | **2917** | **2750** |

| Hormone | n obs | CV prior | Unit | Biological role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| glucose | 508 | 0.12 | mmol/L | regulation signal |
| insulin | 460 | 0.20 | pmol/L | postprandial |
| GIP_total | 380 | 0.30 | pmol/L | postprandial |
| GLP1_active | 315 | 0.40 | pmol/L | postprandial |
| GLP1_total | 300 | 0.35 | pmol/L | postprandial |
| PYY_total | 215 | 0.35 | pmol/L | postprandial |
| ghrelin_acyl | 199 | 0.45 | pmol/L | fasting |
| ghrelin_total | 192 | 0.40 | pmol/L | fasting |
| PYY_3_36 | 152 | 0.40 | pmol/L | postprandial |
| glucagon | 100 | 0.30 | pmol/L | fasting |
| GIP_active | 96 | 0.35 | pmol/L | postprandial |

\newpage

**Table 2.** Joint-mFPC 6-class classification prevalence per cohort (% median [Q1–Q3] across B = 50 pipeline bootstrap re-fits). Modal class per cohort in bold.

| Cohort | Preservado | Imp. lim. | Impaired | Blunted | Enhanced | Altered |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `no_obese_without_T2DM` | **41.7** [39.5–42.8] | 29.5 [27.5–31.3] | 16.2 [14.8–17.9] | 1.6 [0.9–2.1] | 1.7 [1.1–2.8] | 8.8 [7.3–11.2] |
| Obesity | **33.9** [31.9–36.6] | 29.8 [28.2–31.2] | 22.2 [20.5–23.8] | 3.7 [1.3–6.5] | 2.1 [1.2–5.0] | 6.0 [3.9–9.5] |
| T2DM | 20.2 [18.5–24.0] | 24.2 [21.1–27.0] | **27.8** [25.1–30.0] | 9.0 [7.0–15.8] | 7.5 [1.9–12.9] | 13.8 [10.5–19.2] |
| Obesity_T2DM | 19.4 [16.6–20.8] | 23.9 [22.0–26.2] | **27.6** [25.2–29.6] | 10.9 [0.9–14.9] | 4.9 [0.6–11.1] | 15.0 [11.7–19.2] |
| SG | **25.4** [22.1–29.5] | 24.7 [23.2–27.1] | 22.6 [21.7–25.0] | 2.3 [0.6–8.9] | 4.9 [0.6–13.0] | 14.7 [10.4–20.6] |
| RYGBP | 9.2 [7.6–11.4] | 15.4 [13.6–16.4] | 20.3 [18.8–22.0] | 2.4 [0.4–16.4] | 12.4 [0.5–21.0] | **37.1** [34.4–40.2] |

\newpage

**Table 3.** Formal inference. Panel A omnibus + pairwise Pillai. Panel B top-three per-PC F. Panel C significant-PC coverage (FDR < 0.05).

**Panel A — Pillai multivariate contrasts**

| Contrast | Pillai F | p_perm | p_adj_BH |
|---|---|---|---|
| Omnibus (6 cohorts) | 47.6 | < 0.001 | < 0.001 |
| T2DM vs. ref | **108.3** | < 0.001 | < 0.001 |
| RYGBP vs. ref | 86.8 | < 0.001 | < 0.001 |
| Obesity_T2DM vs. ref | 59.3 | < 0.001 | < 0.001 |
| SG vs. ref | 34.1 | < 0.001 | < 0.001 |
| Obesity vs. ref | 22.5 | < 0.001 | < 0.001 |

**Panel B — Dominant per-PC F statistics**

| Contrast | Dominant PC | Secondary PC | Tertiary PC |
|---|---|---|---|
| RYGBP vs. ref | PC1 (F = **496**) | PC7 (F = 275) | PC4 (F = 244) |
| T2DM vs. ref | PC7 (F = **370**) | PC1 (F = 153) | PC3 (F = 104) |
| Obesity_T2DM vs. ref | PC1 (F = 273) | PC7 (F = 223) | PC2 (F = 147) |
| SG vs. ref | PC1 (F = 139) | PC2 (F = 88) | PC11 (F = 83) |
| Obesity vs. ref | PC1 (F = 102) | PC2 (F = 97) | PC7 (F = 36) |

**Panel C — Coverage of significant PCs**

| Contrast | PCs sig. / 14 |
|---|---|
| Omnibus | **14 / 14** |
| Obesity_T2DM vs. ref | 11 / 14 |
| T2DM vs. ref | 10 / 14 |
| RYGBP vs. ref | 9 / 14 |
| Obesity vs. ref | 8 / 14 |
| SG vs. ref | 8 / 14 |

\newpage

**Table 4A.** PTP/IEP Type I–V distribution per cohort, **without glycemic subclassification**, within the classifiable subset (1600 / 2750 = 58 %). Modal Type per cohort in bold.

| Cohort | I.I | III.I | III.II | IV.I | **IV.II** | V.I | V.II |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `no_obese_without_T2DM` (ref) | 6.8 | 12.8 | 12.5 | **47.8** | 18.8 | 1.2 | 0.2 |
| Obesity | 2.9 | 3.1 | 4.3 | 44.6 | **45.1** | 0 | 0 |
| T2DM | 0 | 0 | **37.0** | 34.5 | 28.5 | 0 | 0 |
| Obesity_T2DM | 0 | 3.7 | 6.7 | 33.7 | **56.0** | 0 | 0 |
| SG | 0 | 2.0 | 0 | 43.0 | **55.0** | 0 | 0 |
| RYGBP | 0 | 0.4 | 0.8 | 22.4 | **76.4** | 0 | 0 |

**Table 4B.** PTP/IEP Type I–V with **glycemic subclassification a/b/c restricted to Types III, IV, V** (Patterns I and II by definition imply preserved glycemia and are not subclassified). Modal subtype per cohort in bold. Subtype a = preserved/recovered; b = lower-tail glucose; c = upper-tail/dysglycemic.

| Cohort | I.I | III.I.a | III.I.b | III.I.c | III.II.a | III.II.b | III.II.c | IV.I.a | IV.I.b | IV.I.c | **IV.II.a** | **IV.II.b** | **IV.II.c** | V.I.a | V.II.a |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ref | 6.8 | 2.8 | 2.0 | 8.0 | 3.2 | 2.8 | 6.5 | 15.8 | 9.8 | 22.2 | 7.8 | 2.2 | **8.8** | 1.2 | 0.2 |
| Obesity | 2.9 | 0 | 0 | 3.1 | 0 | 0 | 4.3 | 2.3 | 0.3 | 42.0 | 1.1 | 7.4 | **36.6** | 0 | 0 |
| T2DM | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 37.0 | 0 | 0 | 34.5 | 0 | 0 | **28.5** | 0 | 0 |
| Obesity_T2DM | 0 | 0 | 0 | 3.7 | 0 | 0 | 6.7 | 0 | 0 | 33.7 | 0 | 0 | **56.0** | 0 | 0 |
| SG | 0 | 0 | 0 | 2.0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 4.0 | 0 | 39.0 | 8.0 | 0 | **47.0** | 0 | 0 |
| RYGBP | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0.4 | 0 | 0 | 0.4 | 3.6 | 0 | 18.8 | 10.4 | 3.2 | **62.8** | 0 | 0 |

\newpage

**Figure 1.** Cohort composition and data-flow characteristics.

![Figure 1](../figures/Figure1_cohort_composition.pdf){#fig-1 width=100%}

\newpage

**Figure 2.** Classification prevalence (joint 6-class, IEP Type I–V), Pillai F, and cross-framework concordance.

![Figure 2](../figures/Figure2_classification_inference.pdf){#fig-2 width=100%}

\newpage

**Figure 3.** Trajectory differences with 95 % simultaneous sup-t bands.

![Figure 3](../figures/Figure3_trajectory_bands.pdf){#fig-3 width=100%}
